title: Is There Investment Value in the Soft-Dollar Arrangement? Evidence from Mutual Funds*
abstract: Combining novel data on analyst employment history and mutual fund commission payments, we show that client funds generate higher returns on stocks for which they have access to research by industry expert analysts. The outperformance is greater when funds are more important clients and cannot be attributed to tipping. Client funds place modestly higher weights on stocks covered by industry expert analysts and allocate more commissions to brokers providing such coverage. For identification, we exploit exogenous analyst coverage disruptions. Our findings contribute to the debate on whether mutual funds obtain any investment value from access to analysts through the soft-dollar arrangement.

author.Fei Xie: University of Delaware and ECGI, USA and Belgium
author.Jinfan Zhang: Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shenzhen, China

article.1. Data and Descriptive Statistics:
JEL G11 - Portfolio Choice; Investment Decisions G14 - Information and Market Efficiency; Event Studies; Insider Trading G17 - Financial Forecasting and Simulation G20 - General Issue Section: Article Editor: Lauren Cohen Lauren Cohen Editor Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar

On January 3, 2018, the landmark Markets in Financial Instruments Directive II (MiFID II) went into effect across the European Union. A major provision of the regulation is that fund companies are required to explicitly disclose and justify their payment for investment research. This requirement rekindles a long-standing debate on whether institutional investors derive any investment value from research by sell-side analysts and what makes analyst research valuable. Surprisingly little is known about these questions despite the substantial amount and a steady increase in the share of brokerage commissions used to pay for sell-side research services.1To date, the only source of sell-side value investigated by the extant literature has been early information leaks (“tipping”) about upcoming stock recommendations, which is a highly unethical practice and importantly, does not require sell-side skill or expertise.2Anecdotal evidence also suggests that only 2%to 5%of analyst reports disseminated by brokerage firms are ever read by their institutional clients, casting further doubt on the investment value of sell-side research.3

In this paper, we examine whether mutual funds generate valuable investment ideas through industry expert analysts employed by their brokers to expand our understanding of the investment value, if any, of sell-side research. The reason for our focus on these particular analysts is of two fold. First, survey evidence highlights the importance of analysts’ industry knowledge for institutional investors. For example,Institutional Investor (II)magazine polls buy-side institutions each year on attributes that make a top analyst. Industry knowledge has consistently been ranked as the most important attribute. Corroborating theIImagazine’s annual polls,Groysberg and Healy (2013)andBrown et al. (2016)present survey-based evidence that portfolio managers and buy-side analysts rely on the industry knowledge of sell-side analysts in making investment decisions.

Second, focusing on industry expert analysts provides us with a methodological advantage. It is empirically difficult to assess the value obtained by funds from sell-side research because the sources of information and inputs behind funds’ individual investment decisions are not observable. An identification strategy that can help overcome this challenge would be to isolate fund transactions that are ex ante most likely motivated by broker-supplied research. Given the strong emphasis institutional investors consistently put on analyst industry knowledge, we hypothesize that portfolio stocks covered by industry expert analysts at the fund’s brokers are most likely to fit this description.

Consistent with the industry surveys, analysts with deeper industry insights can help client funds better understand the operational complexities of portfolio firms, including product lines, procurement channels, marketing, sales, distributional networks, and major customers and suppliers. They can also provide their clients with more in-depth analyses of the macroeconomic trends and industry dynamics and develop more refined stock valuation models, which can inform on client funds’ ranking and selection of stocks within each industry (Boni and Womack 2006;Groysberg and Healy 2013). By focusing on the industry knowledge of analysts employed by the funds’ brokers, we therefore isolate a subset of the funds’ portfolio for which the influence of sell-side research should be most pronounced.

We exploit two novel data sets to examine whether analysts’ industry knowledge generates investment value for their mutual fund clients. The first data set contains biographical information on analysts’ prior employment history, which we use to capture their industry expertise.Bradley, Gokkaya, and Liu (2017)validate this approach by showing that analysts with related preanalyst industry work experience generate higher-quality research and are associated with more favorable career outcomes. The second data set is based on fund companies’ semiannual N-SAR filings, which contain brokerage commission payments for an extensive sample of diversified, actively managed equity mutual funds in the merged Thomson Financial and CRSP Mutual Fund database. We use this information to identify broker-client relationships between brokerage firms and mutual funds. Our final sample contains 4,544 mutual funds and 2,161 analysts from 161 brokerage houses during the period 1999 to 2010.

We begin our analysis by employing awithin-fund calendar-time portfolio methodology, in which we compare the abnormal performance of stocks with industry expert analyst coverage from the fund’s brokers to that of stocks lacking such coverage for the same fund over the same time period. This within-fund-portfolio approach allows us to effectively remove the effects of time-variant and time-invariant fund, fund manager, and fund family characteristics that may be related to investment performance.

We find that client mutual funds generateDaniel et al. (1997)(DGTW) characteristic adjusted monthly returns of 20 basis points (bps) on a value-weighted portfolio of stocks covered by industry expert analysts, which compounds to an economically significant 2.43%over 12 months. This stands in sharp contrast with monthly abnormal returns of only 5 (or−6) bps on stocks that are covered by only other analysts (or no analysts) from the fund’s brokers. The differences in the abnormal returns on stocks covered by industry expert analysts and other analysts is statistically significant and economically important, supporting the notion that client mutual funds earn higher abnormal returns on their investments through industry knowledge obtained from sell-side analysts with soft-dollars.

An alternative explanation for these performance differences is that industry expert analysts in general may have a superior ability to identify higher performing stocks, and therefore, the result that holdings covered by industry expert analysts tend to outperform would hold regardless of whether the industry expert analysts are from the fund’s brokers. To address this concern, we compare the same fund’s performance on stocks covered by expert analysts from the fund’s brokers versus the stocks covered by expert analysts from other brokers. We find that funds earn significantly positive abnormal returns on both sets of stocks, but the abnormal returns are much higher on the first set of stocks than on the second set of stocks (20 bps vs. 4 bps), and the difference is statistically significant. This underscores the importance of access to premium analyst services, such as high-touch meetings and phone calls, and suggests that the value generated through soft-dollar arrangements goes beyond that obtained from publicly available research published by the analysts. Interestingly, we also find that even among mutual fund clients of the same broker, those paying more commissions, that is, VIP clients, earn even more pronounced abnormal returns on their investments in stocks receiving industry expert analyst coverage, presumably because they have better access to analyst research services compared to non-VIP clients.

In additional analysis, we examine whether the investment value of analyst industry expertise varies across analysts and stocks. We find that industry expert analysts with higher-quality industry experience confer even greater benefits to client funds. We measure the quality of analyst industry experience in two ways. One is based on the characteristics of analysts’ prior industry employment, including whether they held managerial positions, worked for multiple firms in the industry, or worked for at least one publicly traded firm in the industry. The other is based on the performance of analysts’ earnings forecasts and stock recommendations. The investment value of analyst industry expertise is also more pronounced for fund holdings that move more in synch with their corresponding industries. Finally, our industry experience measure does not simply proxy for analysts’ ability to provide superior management access to mutual fund clients; industry expert analysts without professional connections to coverage firms’ executives or those who do not appear to receive favorable treatment by coverage firms continue to add economically important investment value to client funds.

We buttress the holdings-based performance results by investigating the abnormal profitability of client trades using the aforementioned within-fund-portfolio approach. We find that buy (sell) trades executed by client mutual funds on stocks covered by industry expert analysts are associated with significantly positive (negative) monthly abnormal returns in the following quarter. A long-short portfolio constructed using these buy and sell trades earns significant monthly abnormal returns of 27 bps. These returns are also more pronounced than the monthly abnormal returns of similar long-short portfolios constructed on stocks with only other types of analyst coverage. The trades-based results continue to hold when we use more granular transactional-level trade and commission information from Ancerno Ltd. Once again, client funds with better access to premium research services earn even higher abnormal returns with their trades.

To help establish causality, we examine a dynamic setting that utilizes shocks to a fund’s access to sell-side research created by analyst departures. Using a difference-in-differences (DiD) approach, we exploit time-series variations in fund-stock-analyst pairs and find that client funds experience declines in trading performance on stocks losing analyst coverage from fund brokers, compared to other stocks in the same client funds’ portfolio. More importantly, when we differentiate between different types of analysts, we find that the marginal impact of industry expert analyst departures on the client funds’ trading performance is more pronounced than that of other analyst departures. We obtain similar results using a potentially more exogenous experiment that focuses only on terminations of analyst coverage due to broker mergers, analyst retirements and deaths.

A potential concern about our findings is that the relation between analyst coverage and client fund performance in the covered stocks could be driven by unethical information leaks, such as analysts tipping their clients on upcoming recommendation changes, a controversial practice widely investigated in the literature (e.g.,Irvine, Lipson, and Puckett 2007;Juergens and Lindsey 2009;Xie 2014). However, it is important to note that information leaks do not require analyst industry expertise and therefore are unlikely to explain the differential effect of industry expert analysts relative to other analysts. Nonetheless, we provide additional analyses using daily institutional transactions from theAncerno Ltd.database to further address this concern. In particular, we show that our results are not affected when we exclude institutional transactions taking place beginning 5 days prior to the public release of analyst recommendation initiations, upgrade/downgrades, and revisions (i.e., trades potentially motivated by analyst tipping). This evidence lends further credence to the view that sell-side research provided by industry expert analysts has investment value for institutional clients.

The superior investment returns on fund holdings covered by industry expert analysts from fund brokers raise several follow-up questions: First, do mutual funds allocate more of their portfolios to stocks on which they have access to industry expert analysts? Second, do mutual funds allocate more commissions to brokers that provide more industry expert analyst coverage on the funds’ portfolio holdings? Third, why do we continue to observe funds route trades on stocks to brokers that do not provide industry expert analyst coverage on these stocks? Finally, does the investment value of industry expert analysts to mutual funds extend beyond the stocks they directly cover?

To answer the first question, we compare funds’ portfolio weights on stocks with access to industry expert analysts to those without such access. Our results show that mutual funds exhibit a modest bias in favor of stocks for which they have such access. To establish a more direct link, we employ the aforementioned DiD approach based on coverage terminations due to analyst departures. Our results continue to hold.

To answer the second question, we perform multivariate regression analyses of funds’ allocation of commissions across brokers. We find that mutual funds reward brokers providing more industry expert analyst coverage with significantly higher commissions. Economically, a one-standard-deviation increase in the percentage of a fund’s portfolio stocks covered by industry expert analysts is associated with 0.04%(approximately 7%of the unconditional mean) higher relative broker share of trading commissions from the fund in the following year. The economic impact of industry expert analyst coverage on brokerage commission market share is larger than any other analyst characteristics. Our results are robust to the same identification strategies used in the investment performance analyses.

To gain a better understanding of the third question, we investigate the determinants of mutual funds allocation of stock-level trading and commission across brokerage houses. Examining the fund-broker pairing at the individual stock level, we find that when there is a shortage of industry expert analysts for a stock from available brokers, funds are more likely to allocate trades on the stock to a broker providing only nonexpert analyst coverage. Such instances are also more likely when the broker offers industry expert analyst coverage on a greater percentage of the rest of the fund’s portfolio. This is consistent with the notion that client funds may indirectly reward brokers for their industry expert analyst coverage through channeling trades on other stocks (O’Brien and Bhushan 1990;Irvine 2000;Maber, Groysberg, and Healy 2014).

Finally, for the last question, we uncover interesting and nuanced evidence of expertise spillover indicating that the value of access to industry expert analysts goes beyond directly covered stocks. Specifically, using our within-fund approach, we find that funds are able to generate significantly higher abnormal returns on stocks that arenotdirectly covered by industry expert analysts from their brokers but operate in the industry covered by such analysts. Crucially, this result only holds for stocks in more homogenous industries with higher earnings and stock return synchronicity. This is consistent with our earlier finding that analysts’ industry knowledge is more transferrable within industries where firms are more similar in their economic fundamentals.

Our study makes three main contributions to the literature. First, to our best knowledge, we provide the first evidence that industry expert analysts provide valuable investment ideas to mutual funds. The investment value is much stronger when industry expert analysts are employed by the funds’ brokers; that is, when funds are able to obtain premium research services through soft-dollar payments, and it is even more pronounced when funds are more important clients of brokers employing industry expert analysts. By highlighting analyst industry knowledge as a major piece of the information mosaic that funds use to make investment decisions, our study represents an important first step toward answering the question whether buy-side investors obtain any investment value from sell-side research. In addition, we add to the literature on information flow from economic agents to mutual funds,4and contribute to the recent debate on whether sell-side analysts research on firms is largely “information-free.”5

Second, we conduct the first in-depth analysis of the impact of analyst industry knowledge on brokers’ trading commission market shares. While institutional investors consistently view industry knowledge as the most important quality for analysts, the extant literature is silent on whether analyst industry knowledge actually affects any economic decision making in the relationship between institutional investors and brokerage houses. We show that industry expert analysts help their brokers attract greater commission revenues from mutual funds. As such, we shed light on the economics of the investment research industry and extend a growing academic literature on the determinants of order flow and trading commission allocations.6In this sense, our paper is related to contemporaneous work byDi Maggio, Egan, and Franzoni (2022), who analyze institutional investors’ choices of nonelectronic brokers. They find that the per-share commission rates paid by institutional investors to brokers are related to various potential sources of value, such as execution costs, execution quality, access to research, and access to information on order flow.

Third, our results have the potential to help us understand some of the changes expected to take place as a result of MiFID II. For instance, we find that in contrast to industry expert analysts, access to other analysts is not consistently associated with funds generating abnormal performance on stocks covered by these analysts. At a minimum, this suggests that at least some analysts’ research does not appear to generate investment value for their clients. Therefore, our finding on the investment value of analyst industry expertise is consistent with the trend of brokerage firms to become more specialized and develop deep sector expertise,7and the increased exits of less experienced analysts from the sell-side research profession following MiFID II (e.g.,Fang et al. 2020;Guo and Mota 2021). Our methodology of identifying the investment value of sell-side research and the evidence we produce can serve as a basis for the pricing of sell-side research, which is necessary for mutual funds to fulfill MiFID II’s requirement of disclosing and justifying payments for investment research. Because our tests cannot capture all analyst research driven trades, we likely underestimate the value of their research for institutional investors.
The data set employed in this study is constructed from a variety of sources. First, we obtain broker trading commission allocation data from semiannual N-SAR filings for an exhaustive sample of domestic equity mutual funds during the period of 1999 to 2010. Within each N-SAR filing, registered investment companies are required to provide information on the names of 10 brokerage firms to which they paid the most trading commissions during the reporting period, along with the amount paid to each corresponding broker. Brokerage commissions are reported at the series level in N-SAR filings. A series represents a subset of funds of a given family. Therefore, mutual fund families typically file several N-SAR forms for the same time period: one for each series. We use a proration algorithm to allocate brokerage commissions paid at the series level down to the fund level followingEdelen, Evans and Kadlec (2012).

We start by manually matching the names of mutual funds in the N-SAR filings to those in the CRSP Mutual Fund Database. We then merge the CRSP Mutual Fund Database with the Thomson Financial’s CDA/Spectrum Mutual Fund Holdings Database using MFLinks in order to obtain information on fund holdings. We apply several filters to this merged mutual fund sample. First, we exclude bond, balanced, international, index, and sector funds from our sample. We require that the funds report assets under management and have at least one year of reported returns (Chen et al. 2004). To eliminate incubation bias, we further remove funds with missing names, those with fewer than 10 stocks in their portfolios, and funds for which the year of observations is before the fund’s starting year. Finally, we include funds with multiple share classes only once.

For firms held by funds in our mutual fund sample, we obtain stock returns and financial characteristics from CRSP/Compustat and use the Institutional Broker Estimate System (⁠I/B/E/S)database to identify sell-side analysts providing research coverage. The initial sample of analysts is then merged with I/B/E/Srecommendation files to retrieve each analyst’s last name, first name initial, and brokerage house information. Analysts sharing the same last name and first name initial and working at the same brokerage house are excluded from the sample. Analyst teams are likewise eliminated since I/B/E/Sfurnishes only the last names of analysts assigned to a team.This initial screening criterion yields 7,911 analysts from 1999 to 2010. For each of these analysts, we then search Zoominfo.com, an employment indexing website, to obtain her entire first name. This step returns 4,725 analysts. Finally, we search LinkedIn, the world’s largest professional network, to gather preanalyst industry employment history using the analyst’s first and last name along with the corresponding brokerage house. We are able to locate the profiles of 2,577 unique analysts onLinkedIn, and they are employed by 344 unique brokers. For each of these analysts, we collect detailed information on the names of former employers as well as years of employment at each company regardless of its public/private status. A sell-side analyst is defined as having “related experience” in a firm if one of the analyst’s prior employers and the firm share four-digit Global Industry Classification System (GICS) classification code (Boni and Womack 2006). Analysts with unrelated industry experience and those without preanalyst industry work experience are defined as “other.” For expositional convenience, we refer to analysts with related industry work experience in the stocks they cover as “industry expert analysts.”

Table 1provides descriptive statistics for the overall sample as well as the annual breakdown. We report the number of unique funds and number of unique stocks in the investment portfolios of mutual funds. Our sample contains a total of 4,544 funds investing in 13,634 unique firms. About 32%of stockholding firms have sell-side analyst coverage regardless of trading commission allocations. Focusing on preanalyst industry work experience based on its relevance to stockholding firms’ industries, we find that about 8%of firms are followed by at least one analyst possessing related industry work experience. The table also shows that the percentage of mutual fund holdings receiving analyst coverage generally rises over time, from the 25%–30%range in early part of the sample to about 40%in later years of the sample. This is likely because of the increasing popularity ofLinkedInamong investment professionals including sell-side analysts. While we have no reason to believe this would systematically bias our results, we nevertheless conduct within mutual-fund portfolio analyses and include year fixed effects to control for the potential impact of this time trend on our analyses. With respect to the main characteristics of the funds in our sample (untabulated), the average fund has total net assets (TNA) of $392.99 million, a turnover ratio of 94.55%per year, an expense ratio of about 1.3%as a fraction of year-end TNA, and an age of 8.25 years.

article.2. Fund Performance on Portfolio Stocks:
In this section, we ask whether mutual funds obtain valuable information from sell-side analysts who can help them make better investment decisions. Our main challenge is that the sources of information that motivates fund investment decisions are not directly observable. To overcome this challenge, we focus on funds’ investments in stocks covered by industry expert analysts employed by their brokers, because these portfolio choices are, ex ante, most likely to be motivated by broker-supplied research.

Specifically, we use a standard calendar-time portfolio methodology and compare the holdings- and trades-based performance of stocks in a mutual fund’s portfolio that receive industry expert analyst coverage from the fund’s brokers to that of stocks in thesamefunds’ portfolio that do not receive such coverage. Thiswithin-fundcomparison of stocks allows us to effectively remove time-variant and time-invariant fund, fund manager, and fund family characteristics that may be related to the investment performance of portfolio holding stocks.

2.1 Holdings-based performance

We begin our analyses by evaluating holdings-based performance. In our baseline analyses, we use Daniel et al.’s (DGTW 1997) characteristic adjusted returns to measure the abnormal holdings performance of stocks in a mutual fund’s portfolio. Specifically, at the beginning of each quarter, we classify each fund’s stock holdings according to research coverage by analysts from brokers receiving trading commissions from the fund. We first construct two main portfolios for each fund: one portfolio consisting of stocks covered by any analysts from the fund’s brokers, and another portfolio consisting of stocks with no analyst coverage from the fund’s brokers. We further split the first portfolio into two additional portfolios based on whether a stock is covered by industry expert analysts from the fund’s brokers or only other analysts from the fund’s brokers. Once a stock is assigned to one of the above portfolios, we hold that stock in the portfolio for the entire duration of the quarter and rebalance these portfolios at the end of each quarter in accordance with updated information on analyst coverage and fund portfolio holdings.

To compare the performance of these portfolios of fund holdings with different types of analyst coverage, we calculate monthly value-weighted average returns for each portfolio in each fund-quarter using the dollar value of the holdings as weights. For portfolios with the same type of analyst coverage, we then calculate the weighted average of these portfolio returns across funds in each quarter, using each fund’s TNA as the weight.

Panel A ofTable 2reports the within-fund portfolio holdings results. In columns 1 and 2, we report the performance of the portfolios of stocks with and without analyst coverage from the fund’s brokers. Column 3 shows the difference between the performance of these two portfolios. The results illustrate that the portfolio of stocks with no coverage underperform the coverage portfolio. The raw and abnormal return differences between these portfolios are statistically significant (19 and 11 bps, respectively).

More importantly, when we partition each fund’s coverage portfolio based on whether a stock is covered by industry expert analysts from the fund’s brokers, we find that the value-weighted portfolio comprising only stocks covered by industry expert analysts earns significant raw and abnormal returns (column 4). For instance, the corresponding portfolio’s DGTW monthly alpha is 20 bps, which compounds to an economically significant 2.43%over 12 months. In contrast, the DGTW monthly alpha generated by the portfolio of stocks covered by other analysts only is much smaller in economic magnitude (5 bps in column 6). In addition, columns 5 and 8 show that the abnormal returns of fund holdings covered by industry expert analysts from fund brokers significantly exceed those of other stocks (either those without analyst coverage or those covered only by other analysts). These results suggest that access to analyst industry expertise helps client mutual funds make better investment decisions and deliver higher abnormal returns on the portfolio holdings these analysts cover.8

An alternative explanation for the performance differences in panel A ofTable 2is that industry expert analysts in general may have a superior ability to identify higher performing stocks, and therefore, a fund’s holdings covered by industry expert analysts tend to outperform the funds’ other holdings, regardless of whether the industry expert analysts are from the fund’s brokers.9To rule out this possibility and also directly isolate the value of premium analyst research services mutual fund clients obtain through brokerage commission allocations, we construct another portfolio that consists of a fund’s holdings covered only by industry expert analysts who arenotemployed by the fund’s brokers (industry expert analysts from other brokers). This portfolio has a DGTW monthly alpha of 4 bps (column 2 ofTable 2, panel B), which is much lower than the 20 bps monthly alpha of the portfolio of fund holdings covered by industry expert analysts from fund brokers (column 1 of panel B). The difference is statistically significant (column 3 of panel B). Therefore, these results highlight the value of premium research services provided by industry expert analysts for mutual funds and the importance of access to such services secured through brokerage commission allocations (Valentine 2011;Groysberg and Healy 2013).

Related to the point above, we further explore whether a broker provides all of its clients the same level of access to its analyst research services. We hypothesize that more important clients have superior access to the broker’s premium research services. To test this conjecture, we classify a fund as a broker’s VIP client if its commission payment to the broker is consistently above the median among all the broker’s clients in the past five years.10Using this classification, we partition the portfolio of fund holdings covered by industry expert analysts from fund brokers into two subportfolios based on whether or not the industry expert analysts are from brokers where a fund is a VIP client. Columns 4 and 5 in panel B report the raw and abnormal monthly returns of these subportfolios. We find that both subportfolios generate significantly positive monthly alphas, but importantly, the monthly DGTW alpha is significantly higher by 52 bps for the subportfolio of fund holdings covered by industry expert analysts from brokers where the funds are VIP clients (column 6).

Our results on the importance of access to premium research services through commission allocation could be subject to an alternative explanation based on reverse causality.11Specifically, if funds are able to identify higher-quality industry expert analysts, they may simply follow the publicly available recommendations issued by such analysts rather than paying for the premium research services offered by these analysts to make investment decisions. To the extent that these analysts’ publicly available recommendations help funds earn higher abnormal returns on their holdings, funds decide to reward them by directing more trading commissions to the brokerage firms employing them. We believe this explanation is unlikely to be responsible for our results for two reasons. First, stock recommendations of sell-side analysts are available in the public domain through third-party data vendors (e.g., I/B/E/S, Thomson Reuters Eikon, Capital IQ). Therefore, we believe that funds may not have any incentives to reward these superior industry expert analysts and their employers, as they can obtain the stock recommendations issued by these analysts from public sources and still benefit from trading through venues offering the best execution.

Second, we conduct additional analyses to directly address this concern by eliminating stock-quarter observations that are ex ante most likely to be motivated by publicly available analyst recommendations. In particular, we first identify quartertduring which sell-side analystkrevises her recommendation for stockj⁠. We then exclude mutual fund holdings of stockjin quartertfrom our sample, reaggregate all remaining stock holdings for each mutual fund, and compare the investment performance of the aforementioned portfolio holdings of mutual funds with different types of analyst coverage.12

If the superior investment performance generated on industry expert covered stocks simply originates from publicly available analyst stock recommendations, then we should not expect mutual fund clients to generate better returns on these stocks after we eliminate fund-stock quarters accompanied by recommendation announcements. Alternatively, if our main results are indeed related to the superior industry insights obtained from industry expert analysts, then we would expect mutual funds to continue generating better returns on portfolio holdings covered by such analysts after we eliminate these fund-stock quarters. Results inInternet Appendix Table A2are consistent with the latter view. All of our previous findings continue to hold, including the abnormal returns difference between portfolios of fund holdings covered by expert analysts from fund brokers and those from nonfund brokers, and the abnormal returns difference between portfolios of fund holdings covered by expert analysts from brokers where funds are VIP clients versus non-VIP client. In addition, the economic magnitude of the abnormal returns are very similar to those reported inTable 2. Overall, these results suggest that the publicly available stock recommendations are unlikely to account for any substantial part of the investment performance differences we document.

2.2 Cross-sectional variations in the investment value of analyst industry expertise

In this section we explore cross-sectional variations in the investment value that mutual funds receive from access to industry expert analysts. First, we differentiate among industry expert analysts based on the quality of their industry knowledge, which we use five proxies to capture. In panel A ofTable 3, the differentiation is based on whether the analyst held a managerial position in his/her prior industry employment, whether the analyst worked at multiple firms in the industry, and whether the analyst worked for at least one publicly traded firm in the industry. Our conjecture is that industry expert analysts’ work experience at a managerial level, at multiple firms, and at publicly traded firms (which presumably are larger and more complex), can give them a richer and deeper understanding of an industry.

In panel B ofTable 3, the differentiation is based on the relative accuracy of the analyst’s earnings forecasts and the abnormal returns generated by the analyst’s stock recommendations. Our conjecture is that industry expert analysts who make superior earnings forecasts and stock recommendations may have a better understanding of their industries and can provide deeper industry insights to aid in their institutional clients’ investment decisions. To test our conjectures, we first partition industry expert analysts based on each of the above five proxies. We then evaluate the abnormal return performance of portfolios of fund holdings covered by each subset of industry expert analysts from fund brokers. The results in panels A and B ofTable 3show that each portfolio is associated with significantly positive raw returns and abnormal returns. More importantly, consistent with our conjecture, mutual fund clients earn even higher returns on portfolios of stocks on which they have access to industry expert analysts with higher quality industry knowledge, and the difference is statistically significant at conventional levels for all partitions.

Next, we investigate whether industry expert analysts’ research translates into greater competitive advantages for funds when investing in firms whose operations move more in synch with their corresponding industry fundamentals. The idea is that the experience and knowledge obtained by analysts from working for some firm(s) in the industry are more applicable to peer firms in more homogeneous industries where firms share more commonality in their fundamentals. To examine this possibility, we partition fund holdings covered by the fund broker’s industry expert analysts based on whether a stock’s earnings per share (EPS Synch) and stock return (Ret Synch) synchronicities are above or below the respective sample medians (Parrino 1997;Merkley, Michaely, and Pacelli 2017). Consistent with our conjecture, panel C ofTable 3shows that while the monthly alphas are significantly positive for all subportfolios, they are significantly higher for those consisting of stocks with above-median earnings and stock return industry synchronicity (columns 1–3 and 4–6, respectively).

In addition to deeper industry understanding and insights, prior industry work experience may enable industry expert analysts to provide mutual funds with superior access to portfolio firms’ management. While superior corporate access and industry knowledge are not mutually exclusive channels through which industry expert analysts can add investment value to their mutual fund clients, a potential concern is that our results may be mainly driven by superior corporate access rather than industry knowledge. Therefore, we next investigate the marginal impact of corporate access on the value of these analysts’ research for client funds. In particular, we consider several measures to capture analysts’ ability to provide superior corporate access to mutual fund clients. Our first measure is an indicator that takes the value of one if an analyst shares a professional connection with the coverage firm’s management defined based on overlapping employment between industry expert analysts and executives at coverage firms (Bradley, Gokkaya, and Liu 2020). Our second measure is an indicator that takes the value of one if an analyst is given the opportunity to ask the first question on an earnings conference call hosted by the coverage firm. Our last measure of access to management is an indicator for whether an analyst publishes a takeaway report inThomson Reuters Investextabout her one-on-one meeting with firm management. We partition the portfolio of fund holdings covered by industry expert analysts based on analysts’ access to a firm’s management. Panel D ofTable 3shows that funds earn significantly higher monthly abnormal returns on stocks covered by industry expert analysts who also have better access to the management of the firms (columns 3, 6, and 9). Importantly, industry expert analysts without greater access to firm management still contribute to better fund investment performance on stocks they cover (columns 2, 5, and 8). Overall, the evidence suggests that the investment value of industry expert analysts to mutual funds arise from both their industry knowledge and their access to firm management.13

2.3 Trading-based performance

In this section, we complement the holdings-based performance analyses in previous sections by examining the performance of trades within each fund’s portfolio. To the extent that mutual fund managers capitalize on time-varying investment opportunities in the stock market using research from industry expert analysts, trading-based analyses have the potential to better identify the impact of sell-side research for mutual funds.

Toward this end, we compare the abnormal performance of buy and sell trades by each fund. Specifically, for each holding quarter, we classify a stock as net buy (sell) if the change in the portfolio weight is positive (negative) from the beginning to the end of a quarter, where beginning quarter (lagged) portfolio weights are adjusted to price changes to avoid classifying mechanical weight changes driven by price fluctuations as trades (Kacperczyk, Sialm, and Zheng 2005). Next, for each fund, we distinguish among stocks based on the type of analyst coverage they receive and separately place them into net buy and sell portfolios. Across all funds in our sample, on average 848.65 unique stocks covered by analysts from the funds’ brokers are traded in a given quarter, of which 98.58 are covered by industry experts. For the average fund, this translates into 13.13 covered stocks and 1.80 industry expert covered stocks from the fund’s broker, respectively. We then compute the value-weighted returns of each portfolio using the dollar value of purchased or sold shares as the weight and report abnormal returns from a hypothetical portfolio strategy that goes long on buys and short on sells. The portfolios are then rebalanced at the end of each quarter according to the updated trade direction on each stock in each fund’s portfolio and the type of analyst coverage from funds’ brokers.

Panel A ofTable 4presents the results of our trading-based analyses. A long-short portfolio strategy (buys minus sells) entirely comprising stocks covered by industry expert analysts from fund brokers earns an average DGTW abnormal monthly return of 27 bps (column 1). Both the long and short portions of the portfolio contribute to the abnormal returns, as the stocks the funds buy (sell) earn a statistically significant DGTW monthly alpha of 18 (⁠−9) bps in the next quarter when they are covered by industry expert analysts from fund brokers (untabulated). In contrast, the long-short portfolio constructed using fund trades on stocks with no analyst coverage or only other analyst coverage from fund brokers generates much weaker abnormal returns (5 bps in column 2 and 1 bps in column 3). The long-short portfolio of stocks covered by industry expert analysts from fund brokers outperforms the long-short portfolio of stocks with no analyst coverage from fund brokers by 21 bps per month (column 4), and the long-run portfolio of stocks with other analyst coverage from fund brokers by 25 bps per month (column 5). Both of these differences are statistically significant and economically meaningful. These results suggest that analysts’ industry expertise can also help client mutual funds make more profitable buy and sell trades.

To further demonstrate the importance of access to premium research services through soft dollars, we conduct two additional tests similar to those in panel B ofTable 2. First, we compare the performance of long-short portfolios of buy and sell trades in stocks with industry expert analyst coverage from fund brokers versus stocks with only industry expert analyst coverage from other brokers. The portfolio comprising trades in stocks with the former type of coverage earns an additional monthly alpha of 18 bps (column 6), suggesting that it is not simply industry expert analyst coverageper sebut funds having access to premium research services provided by these analysts that drives our results. Second, among fund holdings with industry expert analyst coverage from fund brokers, we create further differentiation based on whether a fund is a VIP client of a broker. We find that when funds are VIP clients of brokers providing industry expert analyst coverage, the long-short portfolio constructed using their buy and sell trades in covered stocks outperforms non-VIP client stocks by 58 bps per month (column 7). The evidence is consistent with brokers granting their top clients better access to premium research services, which translates into even more profitable fund trades. Finally, as in earlier analyses,Internet Appendix Table A3eliminates stock-quarter observations with stock recommendation revisions to mitigate the potential concern that our trading-based performance results are driven or biased by mutual fund managers’ use of publicly available stock recommendations issued by industry expert analysts. Our results remain robust.

2.4 Identification: Analyst departures

A potential econometric concern with our analyses is that the extent and type of analyst coverage that a mutual fund’s portfolio firms receive from a particular broker may not be random. This poses difficulties to a causal interpretation of the results from the portfolio analyses. For a sharper identification of the value of analyst industry expertise for client mutual funds, we examine a dynamic setting that exploits the time-series variation in fund-stock-analyst pairings due to analyst departures from brokerage houses. If a client fund loses industry expert analyst coverage on its portfolio firms and if research provided by these analysts indeed represents an important source of information for the fund, we expect the fund’s investment performance in the affected stocks to deteriorate. Note that any performance decline in affected stocks should mainly arise from the mistiming of trades: buy-and-hold positions should be unaffected by analyst departures. Therefore, to test our conjecture using this dynamic identification strategy, we examine the performance of trade-based portfolios.

We identify 31,406 (4,145) cases in which a mutual fund experiences a loss of analyst (industry expert analyst) coverage on its portfolio firms due to analysts leaving the fund’s brokers. To eliminate common influences that affect similar stocks at the same time, we employ a difference-in-differences (DiD) approach. Specifically, we construct a long-short portfolio based on a fund’s buy and sell trades on stocks losing analyst coverage (treatment stocks) as well as a long-short portfolio on the same fund’s buy and sell trades on similar stocks without analyst coverage change (control stocks). We then compare the changes in the raw and DGTW returns of the long-short portfolios of treatment and control stocks from yeart−1to yeart+1⁠, with yeartbeing the event year. In particular, we employDaniel et al.’s (1997)algorithm and require that control stocks are in the same size, book-to-market, and momentum quintile as the treatment stocks during the month of June prior to the event and do not experience a change in analyst coverage in the pre- or post-event year.

Panel B ofTable 4presents the results. Focusing on the mean DiD for DGTW abnormal returns, we find that the monthly abnormal returns of the long-short portfolio of stocks where client funds lose access to analyst research services are 21 bps lower in the post-event year (⁠t+1⁠) compared to pre-event year (⁠t−1⁠), and the difference is statistically significant (column 1). In columns 2 and 3, we distinguish among treatment firms based on departing analysts’ industry experience, that is, industry expert analysts versus other analysts. Our results show that the long-short portfolio of treatment stocks losing industry expert analysts from fund brokers is associated with significantly lower monthly abnormal returns in the post-event period compared to the pre-event period (DiD=−90bps). Likewise, for the long-short portfolio of stocks losing coverage by other analysts from fund brokers, the monthly abnormal returns deteriorate by 17 bps from the pre-event to post-event year. More important for our purpose, as shown in column 4, the loss of expert analyst coverage leads to economically and statistically more pronounced declines in client funds’ trading performance in these stocks (Difference in DiD=−73bps).

Panel C repeats the DiD methodology with a subsample of analyst departures that are arguably more exogenous, including those resulting from brokerage house mergers, analyst retirements unrelated to broker events (i.e., age 65 or older), and analyst deaths stemming from the 9/11 terrorist attacks (Kelly and Ljungqvist 2012). Comparing the DiD in abnormal returns of the portfolio of trades in treatment stocks losing industry expert analyst coverage with that of stocks losing only other types of analysts, we find results economically and statistically similar to those reported in panel B.14Overall, the evidence presented in this section lends more credence to a causal interpretation that access to industry expert analysts can improve mutual funds’ portfolio investment decisions.

2.5 Investment value of analyst industry expertise: Ancerno database and tipping behavior

Another plausible concern with our results is that the superior performance that mutual funds generate on stocks covered by industry expert analysts from fund brokers may potentially come from early information leaks about upcoming stock recommendations (“tipping”). The practice of tipping has been the primary focus of academic work investigating the association between sell-side research and institutional clients. The evidence from the literature has been mixed (e.g.,Irvine, Lipson, and Puckett 2007;Goldstein et al. 2009;Juergens and Lindsey 2009;Christophe, Ferri, and Hsieh 2010;Busse, Green, and Jegadeesh 2012;Xie 2014). While we have no reason to believe that industry expert analysts are more likely to tip their mutual fund clients compared to other analysts and that the investment value of expert analysts is mainly driven by such behavior, for completeness, we provide additional analyses to directly address this issue.

To make sure that our results are not driven by early information leakage on upcoming analyst stock recommendations, we exclude fund trades that are more likely to be prompted by analyst tips, that is, trades that are executed around the release of analyst reports on the stock. Because of the low frequency of holdings disclosures, it is not feasible to apply this data restriction to the Thomson Holdings database. Therefore, in this section we use institutional transaction data obtained from Ancerno Ltd. (formerly Abel Noser), a consulting firm for institutional investors that tracks and evaluates transaction costs.Hu et al. (2018)document that the Ancerno data cover roughly 12%of CRSP trading volume over the period of 1999-2005. Ancerno reports the dates, number of shares, brokerage commissions, and broker name for each trade in the data set, allowing for trade-performance analysis at the fund-stock-broker level. Ancerno contains a total of 166 brokers, 176 unique institutions, and 323 money managers during our sample period.

Our approach is as follows. First, we followPuckett and Yan (2011)and calculate implied quarterly trades by aggregating all transactions for each institution-stock pair and each quarter. The resultant quarterly net trades capture the net change in quarterly holdings and are thus analogous to the trade metrics in our previous analyses. In panel A ofTable 5, we reestimate the trade-based results reported in panel A ofTable 4using the implied quarterly trades from Ancerno to confirm that the two data sources yield consistent results on the impact of industry expert analyst coverage on the abnormal performance of trades executed by client funds. As reported in the panel A, we continue to find that access to industry expert analysts leads to higher abnormal returns from trading. In particular, the long-short portfolio of trades on stocks where funds have access to expert analysts earn statistically significant monthly abnormal returns of 62 bps (column 1). This is significantly higher by 50, 35, and 47 bps than the abnormal returns of the long-short portfolios of trades on stocks not covered by any analysts from the fund’s brokers, stocks only covered by non-industry-expert analysts from the fund’s brokers, and stocks covered by industry expert analysts from other brokers, respectively (columns 2, 3, and 4). Even among stocks for which funds have access to industry expert analysts from their brokers, they earn significantly higher abnormal returns from trades when they are the broker’s VIP clients (column 5).

article.3. Analyst Industry Expertise and Mutual:
In this section, we investigate the impact of industry expert analysts on the weights assigned to portfolio stocks by mutual funds. If access to industry expert analysts helps mutual funds generate higher abnormal performance on the covered stocks, we would expect funds to allocate higher portfolio weights to stocks for which they have such access.15To test this prediction, we follow prior research (e.g.,Cohen, Frazzini, and Malloy 2008) and compute portfolio weights at the stock-fund-quarter level, which are the dependent variable of our analysis. Independent variables of interest are indicators for the types of analyst coverage that stocks receive from the fund’s brokers. We control for the fund’s style,16the stock’s market capitalization, market-to-book ratio, and past-12-month momentum, analyst and broker characteristics, and fund and quarter fixed effects.

Panel A ofTable 6reports the regression results. As seen in column 1, client funds invest an incremental 2.3 bps (2%of unconditional mean) in stocks for which they have access to research by sell-side analysts. Differentiating among analysts based on their industry expertise, column 2 shows that the relation between industry expert analyst coverage and portfolio weights assigned to fund holdings is indeed larger (2.2 bps) than that for other analysts (1.4 bps).17

To establish a clearer causal link between analyst coverage and the portfolio weights the fund places on stocks, we use the previously discussed DiD specifications and investigate the impact of analyst departures from brokers on the changes in portfolio weights assigned to treatment stocks by mutual funds. Panel B ofTable 6presents the results. In column 1, we find that funds significantly reduce the portfolio weights on stocks losing analyst coverage (treatment stocks) from fund brokers by an average of 3 bps from the year before to the year after the analyst departure. On the other hand, control stocks experience no significant changes in portfolio weights. Not surprisingly, the difference in portfolio weight changes between the treatment and control stocks is statistically significant.

We then differentiate among treatment stocks based on the type of lost analyst coverage. We find that on average, funds reduce portfolio weights on stocks losing industry expert analyst coverage from fund brokers by 6.6 bps and the weights on stock losing other analysts by only 2.1 bps (columns 2 and 3). The difference in portfolio weight changes experienced by the two groups of stocks is statistically significant (column 4). Given that control stocks experience no changes in portfolio weights, the difference in DiD between stocks losing industry expert analysts and those losing other types of analysts continues to be significant. For even more robust identification, we repeat the above analyses by focusing on potentially more exogenous analyst departures due to broker mergers, age-driven retirements by analysts, and analyst deaths. Our results continue to hold (see panel C).

One possible implication of the results presented in this section is that fund managers may simply invest more time and effort in obtaining information on stocks covered by industry expert analysts (given the relatively higher portfolio weights on such stocks) and this may potentially explain the abnormal investment performance documented in Section 3. To address this concern, we followFich, Harford, and Tran (2015)and identify stocks in which a fund’s dollar investment is above the median within the fund’s portfolio. Even within this subset of fund holdings, which are expected to receive more attention and efforts from fund managers, we continue to find significant evidence of higher abnormal returns accrued to holdings and trades in stocks for which funds have access to industry expert analysts from their brokers (seeInternet Appendix Table A4).
article.4. Buy-Side Commission Allocations and I:
Having shown that mutual funds derive significant investment value from industry-expert analysts at their brokers, we now ask whether mutual funds reward brokers providing industry expert analyst coverage on their portfolio holdings with a greater allocation of trading commissions.

4.1 Baseline regressions

In similar spirits to prior work (e.g.,Green et al. 2014), we define a broker’s relative commission share from a mutual fund as the total commissions allocated to brokerjby mutual fundiduring periodtscaled by total commissions paid by mutual fund i to all brokers during the same time period (%broker share). We estimate OLS regressions of a broker’s relative commission share from a mutual fund on analyst coverage provided by the broker for the fund’s stockholding firms. The primary independent variables of interest are the percentages of the fund’s portfolio firms covered by various categories of analysts employed by the broker, including all sell-side analysts (%analyst coverage), analysts with industry expertise(⁠%industry expert analyst coverage), and other analysts (%other analyst coverage).

We control for a comprehensive set of variables that prior literature suggests can be related to brokerage commission allocations. They include the one-year lagged share of the fund’s commission allocated to the broker (%lag (broker share)), the broker’s size (Top-10 broker), whether the broker and the fund share the same industry specialization (Same industry expertise), the percentage of the fund’s portfolio holdings with investment banking relationship with the broker (⁠%affiliated investment bank), the percentages of the fund’s portfolio holdings covered by the broker’s All-star analysts (%all-star analyst coverage), analysts with high general forecasting experience (%high general experience analyst coverage), analysts with large coverage portfolios (%large portfolio analyst coverage), and analysts with high frequency and timeliness rank for earnings forecasts (%frequent forecaster analyst coverageand%leader analyst coverage).18We also control for broker and year fixed effects, and adjust standard errors for heteroscedasticity and fund-level clustering. To mitigate reverse causality concerns, we lag independent variables by one year.

Table 7reports the regression results. In column 1, the key independent variable is%Analyst Coverage. Consistent with mutual fund commission allocations responding to analyst research, we find that the coefficient estimate on%analyst coverageis positive and significant.19The direction and significance of the control variables are roughly in line with the evidence reported in prior work. For instance, we find that the prior-period commission share is economically the most important determinant of the current-year commission allocations (Goldstein et al. 2009). Brokerage houses affiliated with investment banks that have underwritten equity offerings for more firms in a mutual fund’s portfolio also receive larger commission shares.

In column 2, we decompose total analyst coverage into coverage by analysts with industry expertise and other analysts. Our results indicate that that the marginal impact of analyst coverage on brokerage commission shares is more pronounced for industry expert analysts. A one-standard-deviation increase in industry expert analyst coverage translates into 0.04%higher brokerage commissions in the following year, compared to 0.01%for other analysts. We are able to reject the hypothesis that the effect of industry expert analyst coverage is the same as other types of analyst coverage. Moreover, the economic significance of industry expert analyst coverage is on par with other important analyst characteristics.

Because not all industry expert analyst coverage generates the same investment value for mutual fund clients, we differentiate among industry expert analysts based on the client funds’ performance on the stocks they cover over the past year. If mutual funds reward brokers with higher commission allocations for providing industry expert analyst coverage on their portfolio holdings, we would expect mutual funds to provide even greater rewards for coverage by industry expert analysts if these analysts helped them generate better abnormal investment performance on the coverage stocks in the past. To test this conjecture, we first calculate fund’s DGTW abnormal returns on stocks covered by industry expert analysts from the fund’s brokers in yeart−1⁠. We then partition%industry expert analyst coverageinto two variables at the median of this measure:%industry expert analyst coverage (Higher past performance)and%industry expert analyst coverage (Lower past performance). We estimate the new specification and present the results in column 3 of panel A inTable 7. We find that both of the newly created independent variables have significantly positive coefficient estimates, but the economic magnitude of the coefficient estimate on%industry expert analyst coverage (Higher past performance)is significantly larger, a finding that is consistent with our conjecture. In sum, while funds reward expert analyst coverage by allocating more commission shares to the analysts’ brokers, we find that the rewards are significantly greater for brokers whose analysts helped the fund earn higher abnormal returns on the stocks they cover.

In the remainder of panel A inTable 7, we perform several robustness checks of our baseline results. Specifically, in columns 4 and 5, we augment our baseline specification by including additional controls proposed by prior studies that may affect commission allocations (e.g.,Irvine 2004;Juergens and Lindsey 2009). In column 6, we further control for mutual fund fixed effects to ensure that our results are not driven by any time-invariant fund characteristics. In column 7, we also include fund-year paired fixed effects, essentially examining the relation between analyst coverage and the commission allocation decision by the same fund in the same year. Our results continue to hold in these specifications.20

4.2 Identification

Similar to our performance analyses, a potential econometric concern with our commission allocation analysis is that the extent and type of analyst coverage that a mutual fund’s portfolio firms receive from a particular broker may not be random. For example, brokers may provide more industry expert coverage for portfolio firms of buy-side clients from whom they historically receive higher commissions. Alternatively, mutual funds may gear their portfolios toward stocks covered by industry expert analysts employed by their brokers. Even though both of these possibilities are consistent with the notion that access to industry expert analysts is valuable to mutual fund clients, they pose difficulties to a causal interpretation of the results from the baseline regressions.

To mitigate endogeneity and reverse causality related concerns, we use a dynamic setting similar to that in our earlier analyses. If a client fund loses industry expert analyst coverage on its stockholding firms and research services by these analysts are indeed valued, we expect this client fund to respond by reducing commissions allocated to the broker losing the industry expert analyst. In an attempt to eliminate common influences that affect similar funds at the same time, we employ a DiD approach. Because of portfolio holding differences across funds, not all client funds of a brokerage firm are affected by the departure of a particular analyst. Therefore, client funds not affected can serve as an ideal control group for the affected client funds, that is, the treatment group. We compare the changes in the treatment funds’ commission allocation share to the broker against the changes in the control funds’ allocation to the same broker. To ensure that treatment and control client funds are similar in the pre-event year, we match treatment funds to control funds by their relative commission shares to the broker, the percentage of their portfolio firms with investment banking relationship with the broker, and the percentage of their portfolio firms receiving analyst coverage from the broker. In particular, we require treatment and control funds to be in the same quartile based on each of these characteristics in yeart−1. We retain the candidate control fund that has the smallest difference from the treatment fund in the percentage of portfolio firms receiving analyst coverage from the broker. We examine the effect of analyst coverage loss on commission allocations by computing the mean changes in broker shares from yeart−1to yeart+1for our treatment funds (treatment difference) and control funds (control difference), and then the difference between the changes for treatment and control funds (difference-in-differences). We also compute the difference in DiD between funds losing industry expert analyst coverage and funds losing other analyst coverage.

Panel B ofTable 7reports the results. We find that treatment funds decrease their brokerage commission shares by 0.27%in the year following the departure of analysts covering their stocks compared to the levels in the preanalyst loss period. This is economically important given that the mean (median)%Broker shareof these brokers is 9.09%(8.20%⁠) for treatment funds in the pre-event year. Conversely, the control funds’ commission allocations to the same broker do not change over the same time period. More importantly, in columns 2 and 3, we decompose the loss of analyst coverage into the loss of industry expert analysts and other analysts. We find that the mean DiD in brokerage commission allocations is−0.57%following the departure of industry expert analysts (other analysts) and only−0.20%in the case of other analysts’ departures. Column 4 in panel B reports the difference in DiD between funds losing industry expert analysts and other analysts and shows that the loss of industry expert analyst coverage leads to more pronounced reductions in brokerage commission allocations (Difference in DiD=−0.37%⁠).21To further bolster our confidence in a causal interpretation, we repeat our analysis using a sample of potentially more exogenous analyst departures, that is, those due to broker mergers and analyst retirement and death. The results reported in panel C ofTable 7continue to show that the marginal impact of industry expert analysts on brokerage commission share is significantly larger in magnitude than that of other analysts (Difference in DiD=−1.09%⁠).22

4.3 Stock-level commission allocation analysis using Ancerno data

In this section, we take advantage of the granularity of the institutional transaction-level data available from Ancerno and examine funds’ commission allocation at each individual stock level. First, we verify our earlier findings based on funds’ N-SAR filings and show that all else being equal, funds are more likely to channel trades on a stock to brokers that provide industry expert analyst coverage on the stock. Specifically, we estimate a logistic regression at the fund-stock-broker level. In this analysis, for each traded stock, we consider all the brokers providing analyst coverage on the stock as alternative avenues for funds to choose from. The dependent variable is an indicator variable that is equal to one if a fund channels a trade on a stock to a broker, and zero for all the other brokers. The key explanatory variable is an indicator that takes the value of one for brokers providing industry expert analyst coverage on the stock (Industry expert analyst coverage). We control for a number of analyst and broker characteristics as well as broker and year fixed effects. Standard errors are adjusted for heteroscedasticity and are clustered at the fund level.Internet Appendix Table A6presents the results. Consistent with earlier results in Section5.1, we find that brokers providing industry expert analyst coverage on a particular stock are more likely to receive allocation of trades on the stock from institutional investors.

Next, we try to understand an interesting fact that we observe from the transactional-level data. That is, funds sometimes route trades in stocks to brokers that do not provide industry expert analyst coverage on those stocks. To shed light on this issue, we first consider the availability of industry expert analyst coverage from other brokers on a particular stock held by a fund and examine how it affects the same fund’s selection of a broker providing only non-industry-expert analyst coverage on the same stock. Our conjecture is that the lack of industry expert analyst coverage provided by other brokers on this stock may make it more likely for the fund to execute trades through brokers providing only non-expert-analyst coverage. In addition, prior research suggests that funds may indirectly compensate brokers for research services on a particular stock by routing trades on other stocks to them (e.g.,O’Brien and Bhushan 1990;Irvine 2000;Maber, Groysberg, and Healy 2014). Therefore, we posit that it is more likely to observe funds allocate trades on a stock to a broker providing only non-industry-expert analyst coverage if the broker offers industry expert analyst coverage on a greater percentage of the rest of the fund’s portfolio firms.

We estimate a logistic regression of the likelihood of a fund allocating commissions on a stock to a broker providing only non-industry-expert analyst coverage for that stock. Our first independent variable of interest captures the availability of industry expert analysts on the focal stock from other brokers (Industry expert analyst-other brokers). It is equal to one if industry expert analyst coverage on the stock exists from other brokers, and zero otherwise. Our second independent variable of interest,%industry expert analyst coverage-other client fund stocks,aims to capture the indirect compensation channel and equals the fraction of other stocks in the fund’s portfolio that receive industry expert analyst coverage from the broker. We control for a set of analyst and broker characteristics as well as broker and year fixed effects.

Table 8presents the results. Column 1 shows that the coefficient estimate onRelated Experienced Analyst-Other brokersis significantly negative. In economic terms, coverage by industry expert analysts at other brokers reduces the likelihood of funds allocating commissions to a broker providing only non-expert-analyst research services by 25.0%⁠. In column 2, the coefficient estimate on%Industry Expert Analyst Coverage-Other client fund stocksis significantly positive, implying that client funds may indirectly reward the brokers for providing industry expert analyst coverage on other stocks in their investment portfolios. These results suggest that trading commission allocations for non-expert-analyst coverage may be likely due to shortage in industry expert analyst coverage available from other brokers as well as indirect compensation for expert coverage offered on other stocks in the same fund’s portfolio.23

Overall, our analyses in Section 5 indicate that institutional investors indeed value the industry knowledge of sell-side analysts and reward brokers for the research services these analysts provide. The impact of analyst industry experience exceeds that of other analyst characteristics, which is consistent with the viewpoints from theIImagazine polls and practitioner surveys.
article.5. Additional Analyses:
5.1 Spillover of analyst industry expertise to noncovered stocks

An interesting question arising from our earlier evidence is whether the investment value of analyst industry expertise extends beyond the stocks directly covered by analysts. This question relates to the nature of expertise possessed by industry expert analysts. Is their industry expertise limited to certain stocks, or does it have a more industry-wide application? To shed light on this question, we first examine a fund’s holding and trading performance on stocks that arenotcovered by industry expert analysts from the fund’s brokers but are in the analysts’ coverage industry. Panel A ofTable 9illustrates that a portfolio consisting of these stocks generates a DGTW monthly abnormal return of only 4 bps, which is statistically insignificant. However, an interesting dichotomy emerges once we consider the degree of homogeneity of each industry. Specifically, in panel B ofTable 9, we perform a within-fund analysis (as in Section 3) and partition industries based on the earnings and stock return synchronicity of industry firms. We find that in high-synchronicity industries, the aforementioned portfolio of noncovered stocks operating in the expert’s coverage industry earns a significantly positive DGTW monthly abnormal return of 21 bps based on earnings synchronicity (55 bps based on stock return synchronicity). In sharp contrast, in low synchronicity industries, the portfolio of noncovered stocks earns an insignificant DGTW monthly abnormal return of 1.3 (⁠−2) bps. The differences in abnormal returns between the portfolios in high and low synchronicity industries are also statistically significant. We obtain similar results when examining the trades-based fund performance on these portfolios (panels C and D ofTable 9). Our findings suggest that analysts’ industry expertise does carry investment value to funds on same-industry stocks that they do not directly cover. However, this only holds for more homogenous industries where firms share more similar economic fundamentals, making analyst industry expertise more transferrable across firms.
