5.2 Potential interaction of sell-side analyst industry expertise with in-house industry expertise

While our findings highlight the importance and investment value of industry expert analysts, they do not represent the only source of industry knowledge for fund managers. Funds may have in-house industry expertise or specialize in certain industries (Cici et al. 2018). For this reason, within-fund performance comparisons will favor stock holdings in their expertise industries. To maintain and amplify such information advantages in industries for which they possess in-house expertise, fund managers may want to have access to all conceivable sources of information, including access to sell side analysts with industry experience. If so, this possibility raises two questions. First, is the superior investment value generated on industry expert analyst coverage stocks simply an artifact of a fund’s in-house industry expertise? Second, how does in-house industry expertise interact with the industry expertise obtained from sell-side analysts?

To answer the first question, we need to establish that the fund’s higher abnormal returns on stocks covered by industry expert analysts are not simply driven by the fund’s in-house expertise. Toward this end, we use the lagged weight of each industry in the fund’s portfolio to determine the strength of its in-house expertise in that industry. We classify an industry as a high (low)-in-house-expertise industry if the fund’s portfolio weight in the industry is above median among all industries the fund invests in. Focusing on stocks in the high-in-house-expertise industry or industries,Internet Appendix Table A7shows that funds continue to generate higher abnormal returns on stocks covered by industry expert analysts than on those without such coverage. This suggests that our results are not simply driven by fund in-house industry expertise and that these expert analysts provide industry knowledge and insights that are distinct from in-house industry expertise.

Next, we examine how these two types of expertise interact. Ex ante, it is not clear whether the marginal value of industry expert analysts is higher for funds with strong in-house expertise in the same industry as the analysts than for funds with weak in-house expertise in that industry. On the one hand, funds with strong in-house expertise can better understand, process, and use the information provided by industry expert analysts. On the other hand, given their strong in-house expertise, these funds may not learn as much new information from expert analysts. Additionally, the stocks in the low in-house expertise industries may represent “best idea” trades, as funds may only invest in these “unfamiliar” industries when the industry experts’ information signal is very precise.

To examine these two possibilities, we perform a within-fund analysis in which we compare the performance of two portfolios. The first portfolio consists of stocks for which a fund has strong in-house industry expertise and access to industry expert analysts, and the second portfolio consists of stocks for which the fund also has access to industry expert analysts but has low in-house industry expertise. We find that both portfolios earn significantly positive abnormal returns, and the difference is not statistically significant (holdings-based: 25 bps vs. 35 bps; trades-based: 33 bps vs. 32 bps).

5.3 Industry selection and timing?

In this section, we delve deeper to explore the underlying mechanisms through which analyst industry expertise helps fund managers generate outperformance on stocks they cover. Specifically, we use the methodology developed byKacperczyk, Sialm, and Zheng (2005)to examine the role of industry selectivity and industry timing in client funds’ abnormal returns on industry-expert covered stocks.

Industry selectivity measures the managers’ ability to select stocks from an industry that will outperform their industry peers. The measure is calculated as the value weighted average difference between the return of stocks covered by industry expert analysts at the fund’s broker and that of a corresponding industry portfolio. In contrast, industry timing captures managers’ ability to select the “right” industries, that is, those that are expected to outperform. The measure is calculated as the average difference between last month’s weight allocated to the stock times the current return on the corresponding industry portfolio and the weight allocated to the same stock five months ago times the current return on the corresponding industry portfolio.

Column 1 ofInternet Appendix Table A8reports the results. We find significant evidence for both the industry selectivity and industry timing channels. We also repeat the analysis for fund holdings that are not directly covered by industry expert analysts but are in their coverage industries. Similar to our earlier spillover results, for these noncovered stocks, we only find significance evidence of industry selectivity and timing if they are from high-synchronicity, that is, more homogenous, industries (see columns 2–5 inInternet Appendix Table A8).
article.6. Conclusion:
Mutual funds are prominent consumers of sell-side analyst research services and allocate billions of dollars in soft-dollar commissions to brokerage houses for premium research services. While soft-dollar payments represent an economically significant cost to fund investors, little is known about the investment value generated by these payments. Exploiting a hand-collected novel biographical data on a large sample of sell-side analysts and mutual fund brokerage commission payment data during the period of 1999 to 2010, we find that mutual funds generate economically and statistically higher abnormal returns on stocks with access to research produced by industry expert analysts compared to stocks that are held by the same fund but lack industry expert analyst coverage. Further results suggest that research by industry expert analysts leads to significantly higher returns for more important mutual fund clients, underlining the value of premium analyst client services for investment performance of mutual funds.

To rule out the possibility that our results are driven by unobservable broker, fund, and stock specific characteristics, we exploit external shocks to analyst coverage arising from analyst job changes, broker mergers, and analyst retirements/deaths. We find that losing analyst coverage on fund holdings reduces fund performance on these affected holdings, but the deterioration in performance is more pronounced in stocks losing coverage by industry expert analysts from fund brokers.

Our analysis reveals cross-sectional variations in the investment value from industry expert analysts. In particular, we find that mutual funds benefit more from having access to industry expert analysts who possess higher quality industry knowledge. Industry expert analysts also confer greater benefits to client funds’ performance in stocks from more homogenous industries. In addition, client funds place moderately higher weights on stocks for which they have access to analyst industry expertise from their brokers.

Finally, we examine whether mutual funds reward brokerage firms for the higher abnormal return they generate from having access to industry expert analysts. Our analysis of funds’ commission allocation data shows that mutual funds allocate larger shares of commissions to brokerage firms that provide industry expert analyst coverage on a greater proportion of fund portfolio holdings. On a more granular level, we also find that funds are more likely to channel trades on a particular stock to brokers providing industry expert analyst coverage on the stock. Overall, our study provides timely evidence in light of the MiFID II regulations and contributes to the discourse on the investment value of sell-side research.

2 Existing studies that examine tipping on upcoming recommendations provide mixed evidence: Irvine, Lipson, and Puckett (2007) and Xie (2014) document institutional clients trade before the public release of analyst recommendations, while Juergens and Lindsey (2009) and Busse, Green, and Jegadeesh (2012) find no evidence of such activity preceding recommendation upgrades. More recently, Birru et al. (2022) document that 14 % of sell-side analysts publish short-term trade ideas and that institutional clients trade on this research product ahead of publication dates. Di Maggio et al. (2019) and Barbon et al. (2019) show that brokers also leak information on order flow.

3 See Timms (2018) .

4 For example, prior research has found that mutual funds benefit from information transfer from in-house research departments ( Cheng, Liu, and Qian 2006 ; Groysberg et al. 2013 ), board members in portfolio firms ( Cohen, Frazzini, and Malloy 2008 ), commercial lending units and loan market ( Massa and Rehman 2008 ; Ivashina and Sun 2011 ), and prime brokers ( Kumar et al. 2020 ).

5 Studies in this area generally focus on public information generated by analysts, such as analyst recommendations, price targets, or other ratings ( Altınkılıç, and Hansen 2009 ; Loh and Stulz 2011 ; Altınkılıç, Hansen, and Ye 2016 ; Li and You 2015 ; Kim and Song 2015 ; Bharath and Bonini 2019 ).

6 Prior studies have identified the following determinants: analyst reputation, optimism, boldness and forecast frequency (e.g., Ljungqvist, Marston, and Wilhelm 2006 ; Jackson 2005 ; Juergens and Lindsey 2009 ), broker size ( Irvine 2000 ; Juergens and Lindsey 2009 ), affiliated investment banking units ( Ellis, Michaely, and O’Hara 2002 ; Choi et al. 2009 ), historical brokerage commission shares ( Conrad et al. 2001 ; Goldstein et al. 2009 ), hosting investor conferences ( Green et al. 2014 ), and All-star analysts ( Di Maggio, Egan, and Franzoni 2022 ).

7 See Bloomberg (2018) .

8 For robustness, in panel A of Internet Appendix Table 1 , we report risk-adjusted returns from the Carhart (1997) four-factor, the Pastor and Stambaugh (2003) five-factor, the Fama-French short-term reversal (six-factor), and the Fama-French long-term reversal (seven-factor) models. These additional specifications also allow us to compare the estimated factor loadings on fund holdings covered only by industry expert analysts to other stocks. We do not find any significant differences (untabulated). In panel B of Internet Appendix Table 1 , we use industry-adjusted returns to measure the abnormal holdings performance of stocks in a mutual fund’s portfolio and continue to find robust results.

9 As an initial check for this possibility, we compare the abnormal performance of stocks covered by industry expert analysts to stocks covered by other analysts regardless of stockholdings by mutual funds. We do not find any significant difference, suggesting that industry expert analysts do not exhibit any tendency to cover better-performing stocks.

10 We obtain similar results when using the past three years or all the years in our sample.

11 We thank an anonymous referee for suggesting this possibility.

12 It is well known that the information content of published sell-side research is relatively short-lived and mutual funds wishing to benefit from analyst recommendations need to react quickly to the announcement of recommendations to generate abnormal returns (see, e.g., Brown, Wei, and Wermers 2014 ; Altınkılıç, Hansen, and Ye 2016 ).

13 We also examine the implications of potential analysts’ bias for the value of their research to client funds. In particular, we consider analysts’ affiliation with brokers that have underwriting relationships with coverage firms ( Lin and McNichols 1998 ; Michaely and Womack 1999 ), their strategic or nonstrategic optimism ( Malmendier and Shanthikumar 2014 ), and their herding tendency due to career concerns ( Hong, Kubik, and Solomon 2000 ). Untabulated results indicate that potential biases of industry expert analysts do not significantly affect their impact on the performance of fund holdings that they cover, suggesting that mutual funds are able to debias the sell-side research received from brokers.

14 As a robustness check, we also focus on the departures of analysts who have exhibited above-average capabilities. The rationale behind this test is that the departure of below-average analysts may actually be the result rather than the cause of buy-side investors being unsatisfied with the research service they received and taking their business elsewhere. Our result remains highly significant after we remove those analysts with below-median earnings forecast accuracy.

15 Client funds presumably can receive either positive or negative opinions on stocks covered by industry expert analysts. However, mutual funds are largely not allowed to take short positions in portfolio holding firms. Therefore, we may not observe fund positions on stocks on which they receive negative information.

16 Fund style is computed as the percentage of a fund’s total assets invested in the style of the portfolio holding stock in question, where style is defined as in Daniel et al. (1997) .

17 Our results are consistent with Cohen, Frazzini, and Malloy (2008) , who show that despite their large outperformance, mutual funds only modestly overweight stocks for which managers have a social connection to corporate board members. In their sample, the average abnormal weight (from the empirical specification that matches ours) is 9.5 bps, which translates into 0.22 % of the fund’s assets being invested in connected stocks. While our average abnormal weight is smaller, approximately 9 % of fund total net assets are invested in stocks with industry expert analyst coverage from fund brokers.

18 We discuss the motivation for including these controls and present their definitions in the appendix and summary statistics in Internet Appendix Table A5 .

19 Similarly, Di Maggio, Egan, and Franzoni (2022) find that institutional investors pay higher commission rates on trades executed through brokers employing more analysts.

20 In unreported analysis, we take into account the dollar investment in each stock in a fund’s portfolio and redefine analyst coverage variables based on the proportion of portfolio dollar investments in stocks covered by each type of analysts. Our results are robust.

21 We also consider the magnitude of loss in analyst coverage resulting from analyst departures and find that the impact of industry expert analyst loss is higher (lower) when a larger (smaller) proportion of mutual fund holdings are affected by this loss.

22 To circumvent the mechanical relation between analyst loss from broker mergers and changes in commission allocation arising from target brokers disappearing following the merger, we focus on the analysts who are employed by the acquiring brokers in year t − 1 and require such brokers to exist in year t + 1 ⁠ .

23 Other possible reasons for funds to not always use brokers providing industry expert analysts coverage for their portfolio firms include (1) for noninformation based trades, for example, routine portfolio rebalancing, funds usually trade through the lowest execution cost brokers, and (2) funds intentionally spread their trades across several brokers to conceal their private information and trading strategy (e.g., Brogaard et al. 2019 ).
